sentences.text: --talking to people there.
And though I said I'm not going to do it, somehow I came out and said that I'm a Holocaust survivor.
And they said they'll get in touch with me.
But they didn't have my address or anything.
So they did get in touch with my daughter.
And she told me that they called her from the thing.
And I told her that I really don't want it.
And I don't-- it's gonna be too painful.
I don't want to go back on it.
And I guess she let it go.
But then somebody from around you called me.
And I told them the same thing.
But they insisted that I should do it.
And I finally said that they should call me back.
And when they called me back, I said OK.
No.
No.
No.
I didn't tell them about it.
I didn't think that it's gonna be really to-- that kind of production.
But I'm gonna do my best.
I hope that it'll help somebody.
Thank you very much for being here.
We'll begin if you can please tell me your full name.
My name is Joyce Lipper.
My maiden name is Maslansky.
February 28, 1925.
I'm going to be 72 in February next year.
I'm born in Klewan, Poland.
Small town.
But we had a big Jewish population.
We had about four synagogues.
Maybe 5,000 or so.
Quite a big percentage.
Because the non-Jewish people mostly lived on the outskirts of town, like farms and stuff.
So I would say maybe 55%, 60% were Jewish.
Business, mostly.
My mother had three daughters.
My father passed away when I was six months old.
Manechan.
And my mother raised three girls by herself.
She had a little grocery store in the house.
Chana.
My sister-- my older sister's name was Shifra.
And my younger sister's name was Mina.
I was the baby, yes.
Not bad, not bad.
I-- we were poor, but I didn't feel it, a lot of family closeness.
We had-- my father's family was an American.
And they helped us a lot.
My older sister, when she became of age, she was-- went to school and learned to be a dressmaker.
And she was helping out.
And we all did little chores.
But it was a happy, good family.
My father's oldest brother went-- came to the United States.
I really don't know exactly how.
But then after he-- another brother and then a sister.
And then they took their mother, they brought over to the United States.
So I really had here in the States my father's family when I came.
I really couldn't say.
I don't know.
My sister, my older sister, they were planning about it that when she'll be old enough that they gonna take her-- bring her to America, yes.
Right.
Yeah.
The family, closeness, had there-- there aunts, uncles, their families.
We were very close, taking care of each other.
And it was nice.
I was young, going to school.
And even if I didn't have much, I didn't miss it.
Yes.
We didn't mix too much, except in school.
But I did have some good friends, non-Jewish people.
Certain things in town I remember they were talking about.
But as I said, I was a young girl.
And I really don't remember having to deal with any of that.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
When the Pole-- the war between Poland and Germany, they-- we started to see people from Germany coming our-- to our part.
Because we were on the closer to the Russian borders, the Ukraine part of Poland.
So there were people coming to our part, special Jewish people.
But then supposedly Stalin made the pact with Germany.
And we became Russians.
That's when wars.
By us, that was when the war started between the German and the Russians in '41.
That's when we really felt it.
That's when it-- the trouble started.
In the summer, the beginning of the summer, I would say maybe June of '41, the Germans came in.
Knowing what to expect from the Germans, we all tried to run away and hide in maybe the small towns.
And they were bombing.
And a lot of people were in the synagogues hiding.
And when they came in that day, they killed 20,000 people.
The Germans.
They bombed the synagogues.
We had one big, big synagogue.
And there were a lot of people there hiding, because the structure was a sound building, a big one.
So they thought that they would be safe there.
I ran away to a small town, like a village, where you expected the Germans not to go through right away from the first came in.
And I ran away with my mother and my two sisters.
That was a little village.
Nowostaw.
We heard from the Polish people, the Jewish Polish people that came to our part of the country, that they kill Jews and they take them to work, hard labor, and all that.
And so we knew a lot what was going on.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
There were people that came and said that they ran away, but their families were killed and that they bombed the city.
I mean, this was a war.
So they bombed the city where a lot of people got killed.
But when they came in, they [? used ?] [? the ?] special look for the Jewish people.
And--
I guess so, when you talk to people that you see that they left everything and were running away.
So you have to believe it.
Yes.
The Russians came in when they divided Poland in half with Germany.
So life was fine.
I mean, there weren't any special luxuries.
But we weren't used to luxuries like we know now.
And it was life going on.
People went to work.
I went back to school.
And--
Yes.
Yes.
We were taught Russian.
And they tried to organize the youth organizations.
And they gave a lot of attention to young people to try to tell them about Russia.
And-- but at that time, being young in school, you didn't mind those things.
Because it was fun time.
It wasn't bad.
No.
No.
They wanted the young people to join the [NON-ENGLISH].
That's the Russian youth organization.

And if you-- if they found out there was a lot of people that were telling on people-- let's say they were rich or they had big business-- those are the people that they use to send away to Siberia, take them away, a lot of non-Jewish people.
Yes.
Yes.
My husband's family had been through that years ago, before the World War II.
My husband's father was sent to the Siberia, because they were rich.
And they-- when the Russians at that time came in in our area, they sent them away.
So we knew a little bit about it.
But I knew a lot of people even, as I said, the non-Jewish people were overnight-- get out and just with trains moved out.
And nobody knew what happened to them.
But I guess-- but after a while, you knew that that's going on.
And was your mother still able to run her grocery store the same?
Not as much as before, because it wasn't available, the products that she was selling.
But we managed, yes.
No.
No.
No.
Yes.
We went to school together.
And I met him in my fifth grade.
We were friends.
We grew up together.
And he was one class higher than I was.
And we were friends.
It was Eli.
No.
No, not really.
We were kids.
And we just hang out together.
And in fact, he came from the richest family in the town.
And I was one of the poorest.
Because a mother-- a woman with three girls by herself wasn't a good thing in Europe at those times.
We really didn't give a chance to do that.
Because, I mean, we were going to school.
And they were trying, you know, to get us to get to know the organizations and so on.
But then the war started again.
When the Germans came in, our town was bombed.
Because we lived on the main road where it was going from the south to the north.
And as I said, it's not just that they bombed the town coming in.
But that first night, they bombed all the synagogues and killed a lot of people.
But they also killed non-Jewish people.
Yes.
About a couple of days later, we all came down.
We knew what went on there, that were-- people were killed and so on.
But we came back to town.
And they-- they put us in a-- in one area, the Jewish people.
Most of our homes were bombed.
There weren't any homes to go to.
But we went five, six, maybe even more, families in one house.
And it was like a ghetto.
But it wasn't under wire or we weren't at that time.
I would say maybe in August or so.
Yes.
Not really, no.
As I said, we lived in a house there, that-- the area that they told us that we should stay in.
We lived with other people, a lot of people, five, six families.
No, not really.
No.
You didn't decide.
You just went where you had a chance to go, where you had a place to go.
A lot of people were-- were killed already.
I lost my younger sister.
So there were just the three of us.
She was killed the first time when they came in.
It would be in July of '41.
I really don't know what happened.
I know that she got killed.
She was-- at the time, she was with the people, where hiding.
And they killed all the people.
She was in town.
With other people, with my aunt and uncle.
And at that time, I lost my cousins.
I lost a lot of family.
We came back to the town.
And we settled down like in the area where they wanted us to live and stayed and go to-- went to work every day.
Fixing the roads, lumber yard.
There was a plant by us where they were making lumber.
Not making lumber, but cutting the lumber trees to-- to boards and things like that.
So we work there and every day came home at night and had very little to eat.
They made like a Jewish committee that they took care of us.
And we used-- they used to give us out bread every day and so on.
But it was already-- we saw that it's-- something is coming.
Because in September that year, they had got together the rest of the Jewish people.
And they said that they-- we heard about it before-- that they're gonna be killing the rest of the Jews.
So that's when--
People were talking.
People were talking.
But-- and they did certain things.
They didn't let you go to work.
We found out afterwards that that day, a couple days already, they didn't let you go to work.
They wanted you to stay put and so on.
So--
Yeah, not to go out from the area.
Except that you-- when you went to work, you were led.
They used to come and ask for a certain amount of people, or men, or women.
And they led us to the-- where they wanted us.
A lot of times they took us with a bus or some kind of truck or something to work.
And--
That we were getting food-- when we went to work, we used to get an extra piece of bread or something else extra.
That's why people went to try to go to work.
I used to go every day to work.
I was a young girl, strong.
And I used to go home and bring the extra piece of bread or whatever I got for my family.
Maybe even more.
Three in a bed [? also. ?]
My mother and sister.
Sure.
Each corner had a bed.
[SIGHS] I don't think there was any to talk about.
No.
No.
No.
We weren't rich people.
We didn't have much to take.
And we didn't have anything.
We had the clothes on our back.
Some people that knew what's coming and saw it and had things.
So they gave it to some non-Jewish people, maybe to hold or-- or put it in their home somewhere where they-- even the home was bombed, they could go in in the-- in the cellar and get it or something.
Yes.
Yes.
We were getting ready for it.
My-- at that time my friend, my boyfriend, I would say, family, they had means.
So they had connections in the big city from our town.
And they said that they're gonna go there, thinking that there, they gonna let the Jews live longer or whatever.
There was a ghetto already there.
Rowne.
That was, like, each state had a capital.
Is that what you say?
And that was the capital of [NON-ENGLISH].
And at that time, my mother with my sister went to the town where my mother came from.
She had her sisters and brothers.
Alexandria.
And I went with my husband-- not at that time, but my boyfriend's-- family.
And we went to Rowne.
About September of '41.
And--
No.
You didn't have to have do anything.
You just had to make sure that you can get out without being caught, if you were caught on the road going to that city.
We walked.
It was 26 kilometers from our town to Rowne.
If you walked and you got caught, you got killed.
We can talk?
Yeah.
Dress up or that they shouldn't recognize that you're Jewish.
Try not to walk during the day.
OK.
